title: Vote of Confusion: Shifting Election Laws Ahead of the Midterms
content:
As Republicans push a sweeping election-law strategy through the courts and Congress, voting rules are shifting late in the cycle. This raises the risk of disruption, mistrust, and fresh legal chaos ahead of the midterms. In a recent case before the Supreme Court, the conservative majority seemed sympathetic to the Republican National Committee's demand to bar certain state laws. These laws currently validate mail-in ballots postmarked but not received by officials on Election Day. If the RNC's argument proves successful, millions of Americans accustomed to voting by mail will need to learn new rules before this fall's midterms.

Currently, Texas and 13 other states allow postmarked ballots to be counted, even if they are only received by couriers on Election Day. Furthermore, 29 states offer that same privilege to military and overseas voters. These established rules could be abruptly terminated by the impending court ruling. Election experts warn that any of these kinds of policy changes are always borne by local election officials who are already stretched thin. If states are forced to change these deadlines before the fall, it will demand a remarkably tight turnaround.

This case is just one of hundreds of lawsuits being litigated that could alter how the upcoming election is administered. The RNC has focused significant time and resources on challenging American election laws to make the system more secure. Their stated goal is to restore voter faith in the electoral process and push the judiciary to clarify contradicting election laws. However, recent primary elections in Texas highlighted how last-minute changes can disenfranchise voters who turn up to the wrong place or at the wrong time. This confusion could be a mere taste of what is to come in November as these legal battles continue to unfold.
